structured_choices.data_source: The data is sourced from electronic health records (EHRs) of approximately 100,000 patient admissions from a multi-site hospital system over the past three years.
structured_choices.label_definition: The label is defined as a binary outcome indicating whether a patient is readmitted within 30 days post-discharge.
structured_choices.split_method: A stratified random split is used, ensuring both temporal and demographic distributions are represented across train, validation, and test sets while aiming to balance readmission class proportions.
structured_choices.preprocessing_fit_point: Transformers for preprocessing are fit on the training data only to avoid any leakage of test information into the model.
structured_choices.features: Features include patient demographics, clinical data, prior admission history, and EHR-derived statistics such as average admission length and frequency of admissions.
structured_choices.model: The proposed model is a recurrent neural network (RNN) with attention mechanisms to model sequential patient history and its impact on readmission.
structured_choices.baseline: The baseline comparison is a traditional logistic regression model using demographic and clinical features, tuned using grid search for hyperparameters.
structured_choices.primary_metric: Recall is chosen as the primary evaluation metric due to its alignment with stakeholders' goal of capturing as many actual readmissions as possible.
structured_choices.model_selection_data: Model selection, including hyperparameter tuning, is performed using the validation set, ensuring the test set remains untouched until final evaluation.
structured_choices.test_set_policy: The test set is used once at the end for final evaluation to prevent any data leakage and ensure unbiased results.
structured_choices.confound_controls: Demographic variables and hospital site identifiers are included as features to control for variability across patient populations and treatment protocols.

design_narrative: This experiment aims to evaluate whether a recurrent neural network (RNN) with attention mechanisms surpasses traditional logistic regression models in predicting patient readmission rates using electronic health records (EHRs) from a large hospital system. Roughly 100,000 patient admissions recorded over the past three years provide a comprehensive dataset with labels indicating whether readmissions occur within 30 days after discharge. The dataset features clinical and demographic information, alongside summarized statistics of prior admissions. A stratified random split ensures diverse and balanced representation of the admission sequences across training, validation, and test datasets, assuming temporal autocorrelation is sufficiently weak. This method accounts for the sequential nature of the data while avoiding excessive leakage of future patterns into training sets. Preprocessing steps—such as normalizing admission frequency and demographic encoding—are fitted exclusively on the training set to mitigate information leakage. The experiment compares two models: an RNN equipped with attention mechanisms, designed to capture sequential interactions within patient data, and a logistic regression model optimized through grid search. A focus on recall, selected as the primary metric, aligns with stakeholders' emphasis on identifying as many true readmissions as possible. Precision, F1-score, and AUROC serve as supportive metrics to further evaluate classification efficacy and calibration. Model selection is conducted solely on the validation set, preserving the test set for a single, conclusive performance assessment. By including demographic and hospital identifiers within the feature set, we mitigate the risk of confounds, ensuring variability across patient populations and procedural differences are accounted for. This experiment, grounded in robust methodology, seeks to deliver actionable insights into optimizing patient readmission prediction strategies.

ambiguous_choice.defensible_rationale: The chosen stratified random split balances both demographic features and readmission class proportions across dataset partitions, which is an appropriate choice if direct temporal autocorrelation effects are minimal, as the hypothesis targets cross-sectional signals within patient records.
ambiguous_choice.legitimate_challenge: Given the sequential nature of the data, strong temporal autocorrelation might exist where patient readmission patterns change over time or are influenced by previous readmissions, potentially resulting in leakage if sequences from the future accidently inform the training data.
ambiguous_choice.empirical_condition: Measure the autocorrelation coefficient of readmission rates across sequential patient visits; if the coefficient exceeds 0.3, indicating a meaningful temporal dependency, adjust to a chronological split to prevent leakage.
